🐂

Bull Theory — Content Playbook

@BullTheoryio · 129.6K followers · Internal guide for all writers

Voice & Core Rules

Voice model
JPMorgan macro note — in plain everyday language
Target reader
Retail investor, 18–40, not a financial professional
✓ Always do
DO
Use exact numbers. Never round or paraphrase. Write "$1.08 trillion" not "just over $1 trillion."
DO
Big foreign currency numbers are fine and look attractive — always include the USD equivalent next to them. ₩305,000,000,000,000 ($220 BILLION) is correct. The zeros stay in the body — not in the hook line.
DO
"BLOODBATH" is an accepted strong word on this account when markets crash hard. Use it.
DO
Write dollar drops as "$4,000 drop" — dollar sign before the number, no minus sign prefix.
DO
Use "reportedly" or "allegedly" for anything unverified. Never state allegations as fact.
DO
Keep paragraphs to one idea each. Short sentences. Direct language.
DO
Stay on-brand: markets, macro, crypto, AI, geopolitics. Off-brand stories stay off the account.
✗ Never do
NO
No poetic closing lines added for rhythm: "One AI announcement changed everything." Delete these entirely.
NO
No "roughly," "around," "approximately" — get the exact figure or don't include it.
NO
No flag images as a second image alongside a chart. A flag adds zero information.
NO
No cropped or cut-off charts. If it doesn't display fully on mobile, reshoot or go image-free.
NO
No editorializing for or against any government or company. Neutral always.
NO
No generational labels. Write "people above 60" not "Baby Boomers."
NO
BREAKING is for live breaking news only — not recaps, milestones, or analysis posts.

Correct number format — from real posts

✗ wrong — minus sign before dollar
"BITCOIN is down -4000$" — minus prefix AND dollar sign after the number. Two formatting errors in one line.
✓ correct — plain number format
"Oil crashed 15.27%" — clean, no minus prefix. Dollar sign always before the number when used.

Post Structure

The 4-part framework — every post follows this
1
Hook — The single most shocking fact. One line. No warmup. No background.Test: if someone reads only this line, do they understand the main event? If not, rewrite it.
2
Context — Exactly what happened. Exact numbers and dates. No editorializing.Example: "The company debuted the Ferrari Luce, a 1,050-horsepower vehicle starting at €550,000."
3
Analysis — Why does this move markets? One or two sentences. Keep the logic simple.Example: "Its 2030 revenue projection came in €800 million below what analysts were expecting."
4
Big picture — Connect to Bitcoin, equities, commodities, or Fed policy. One or two sentences max.Example: "The stock is now down 41% from its February 2025 peak — one of the worst stretches in Ferrari's history as a public company."

Ferrari post — all 4 parts visible (701K views)

① Hook
BREAKING: Ferrari $RACE is down -7.57% today after unveiling its first fully electric car.
② Context
The company officially debuted the "Ferrari Luce," a 1,050-horsepower vehicle starting at €550,000.
③ Analysis
Investors are skeptical that traditional Ferrari buyers want a silent, heavy electric vehicle. Its 2030 revenue projection came in at €9 billion — €800 million below what analysts expected.
④ Big picture
The stock is now down 41% from its February 2025 peak. One of the worst stretches in Ferrari's history as a public company.

The two-line format — works when contrast is extreme (7.8M views)

✓ why it works
$2.9 million vs $5. The gap between those two numbers is so extreme the numbers tell the entire story. No analysis needed. The contrast is the hook.

AI investigative format — thread hook with supporting documents (3M views)

✓ how this format works
Bold claim in the hook, then supporting evidence via screenshots of source documents. Works for investigative stories where the receipts are the proof. Hook must be the most extreme version of the claim.

Investment scenario format — relatable personal hook (1.7M views)

✓ how this format works
Put the reader in the story: "If you invested $50,000..." It makes an abstract number — 3000% — feel real and personal. Always anchor with a specific starting dollar amount.

Breaking News Rules

Format rules — non-negotiable
1
BREAKING is only for live news happening right now. Not recaps, not milestones, not analysis.
2
Start with "BREAKING:" then the headline immediately. No emoji before the word BREAKING.
3
The headline must be the single most important fact — not background, not history. The news itself.
4
Max 2–4 lines of supporting context. No essays. No subheadings inside the body.
5
No bullet points. Prose only.
6
One chart preferred. A second image (person, product shot) is fine if it adds real context. A country flag is never useful as a second image.
7
Chart must be fully visible on a phone screen. If it is cropped or too small to read, don't use it.

Strong BREAKING hooks — learn these patterns

✓ 4M impressions
One line. GDP comparison does the work. No setup needed. Chart + person photo is a valid two-image combo.
✓ exact stat, historical context in the hook
"15.27%" and "2nd highest in 4.5 years" — both in the hook. The historical frame makes the number more shocking.

BREAKING used incorrectly — real examples

✗ BREAKING on a market recap
This was a recap of Asian market losses — not live breaking news. Remove BREAKING. "BLOODBATH in Asian Markets" is a strong hook on its own.
✗ BREAKING on a milestone post
Taiwan hitting $5 trillion is significant but not live breaking news. This is a milestone post — it works without BREAKING.
⚠ Rule of thumb: if the event happened more than 30 minutes ago, or if you are writing a recap — remove BREAKING. The content is still strong without it.

Late Coverage Strategy

If everyone already posted the headline, posting the same thing adds zero value. You need to offer something others didn't — deeper numbers, a new angle, or context that tells a bigger story.
How to add the edge when you are late
1
Go deeper on the numbers.Everyone said "market crashed." You report exactly how much was wiped out, how fast, and how it compares to previous crashes by percentage.
2
Find the downstream effect.Everyone covered the event. You explain what it means for Bitcoin, the S&P 500, a specific sector, or commodity prices.
3
Add unique verified context.A data point or timeline no competing account used. Always verify before posting — never fabricate context to fill space.
4
Use a better visual.If your chart is cleaner, more readable, or shows a longer timeframe than what others posted — that visual alone adds value.

Asian markets reversal — good story, unattractive chart

✗ three compressed mini-charts
Three charts squeezed into one image. Labels are tiny, price levels unreadable on mobile. The story is fine — the visual kills it.
✓ single clean heatmap
One full heatmap. Every stock and percentage readable at a glance. The visual itself is the content.
✓ Fix: Use one chart per post. For multi-market stories, pick the most dramatic chart — then reference the other markets in the text.
✓ Better hook for the Asian reversal post: "Three Asian markets hit all-time highs in the same session, adding $266 billion combined." Lead with the total, then break it down by market in the body.

US stocks post — strong visual, weaker hook (34.5K views vs potential)

⚠ hook issue
"INSANE STRENGTH IN US STOCK MARKET." — this is dramatic language without a specific number. The $360 billion figure is in the second line. It should be in the first line.
✓ Better: "$360 BILLION added to US stocks today as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq opened at the highest levels in history." Lead with the dollar number — that is the hook.

Mistake Library — Every Recurring Error

1
Flag image as second image alongside a chart
Seen in: KOSPI post (South Korea flag)
✗ chart + flag = wasted slot
The South Korean flag image alongside the KOSPI chart adds zero financial information. The flag does not help the reader understand the story.
✓ chart + person = valid second image
Jensen Huang's photo next to the NVDA chart works — his face connects directly to the company story. A person adds human context. A flag does not.
✓ Rule: A second image is valid if it shows a person, product, or graphic directly related to the story. A country flag or generic logo image by itself tells the reader nothing — remove it.
2
BREAKING used when the post is not live breaking news
Seen in: BLOODBATH recap post, Taiwan milestone post, KOSPI ATH post
✗ BREAKING on a recap
Asian markets crash recap written after the session. Not live news. Remove BREAKING — "BLOODBATH in Asian Markets" is already a strong hook without it.
✗ BREAKING on a milestone
KOSPI hitting an all-time high is notable — but it is a milestone post, not live news. Also contains "exploded" which is dramatic language. Remove both.
⚠ BREAKING = live news happening right now. If the event happened more than 30 minutes ago, or if you are recapping — remove BREAKING. Strong numbers stand on their own.
3
Two mistakes in one tweet: cropped chart AND wrong number format
Seen in: Bitcoin crash post
⚠ Mistake A — Cropped chart: The Bitcoin chart is cut off at the top. The highest price levels and the chart title are not visible. On a phone screen this chart is unreadable. Rule: before posting, scroll to the image on your phone. If any part of the chart is cut off, reshoot it.
⚠ Mistake B — Wrong number format: "BITCOIN is down -4000$" has two errors. The minus sign goes before the dollar amount (not after), and the dollar sign belongs before the number. Write: "Bitcoin dropped $4,000 in 60 minutes."
✓ Corrected version: "BREAKING: Bitcoin dropped $4,000 in 60 minutes and broke below $73,000. BTC wiped out $84 billion in market cap in just 2 days, liquidating $650 million in long positions."
4
Using "roughly" or approximate numbers
Seen in: SK Hynix post — "roughly 240%"
✗ "roughly" signals guessing
"The stock is now up roughly 240% in 2026 alone." — the chart literally shows +240% YTD. The word "roughly" was unnecessary and undermines credibility.
✓ every number exact
$387,000 → $14 million → $21 million. Every figure precise. No "roughly," no rounding. The specificity is what makes the comparison land.
✓ Fix: "The stock is up 240% in 2026 alone." If you genuinely cannot verify the exact number, leave it out entirely — do not guess.
5
Raw foreign currency zeros in the hook line
Seen in: NIKKEI post, KOSPI post
✗ zeros in the hook kill readability
¥17,400,000,000,000 in the first line. The reader stops reading before reaching the actual story. Never put a wall of zeros in the hook.
✓ USD in hook, local currency in body
Hook uses clean USD figures: $133B, $70B, $99B. The local currency numbers (NT$, ₩, ¥) appear in the body text where they add flavor without killing the hook.
✓ Rule: Hook always uses USD. Foreign currency with USD equivalent can go in the body lines below — it adds detail there without hurting the opening.
6
Unattractive or messy chart
Seen in: Asian markets reversal post
✗ three compressed charts in one image
Three mini-charts crammed into one image. Tiny labels, unreadable price levels, no clear focal point. Looks unprofessional.
✓ clean single visual
One full-size heatmap. Every stock and percentage readable at a glance. The visual is the story — it does not need explanation.
⚠ Before posting: look at the image on a phone screen. If you cannot read the price levels or the labels clearly, the chart fails. Reshoot with a wider timeframe or a different chart type.
7
Dramatic language in the closing line — added for effect, not information
Seen in: Allbirds post
⚠ "One AI announcement changed everything." — this closing line was added for dramatic effect only. It contains no new information. It sounds like influencer content, not market research. Delete it.
✓ The post was otherwise strong. End on the last factual line: "The company was days away from shutting down entirely before the pivot." That is more powerful than any rhythm line.

Good vs Bad — Visual Comparison

High-performing posts — study these formats

✓ 7.8M — two-line contrast format
$2.9M vs $5. Two lines. No analysis. The gap between those numbers is the entire story.
✓ 3M — investigative hook with evidence
Bold claim in the hook, then source document screenshots as proof. Credibility comes from showing the receipts.
✓ 2.8M — story hook with data twist
Opens with a human story, delivers a data-driven twist that reframes everything. $14M vs $21M is the gut punch.
✓ 444K — large number + heatmap visual
No BREAKING needed. Strong exclamation + ₹5.77 trillion figure + heatmap. The chart carries the post.

Hook: generic opener vs specific number

✗ hook is a label, not a fact
"INSANE STRENGTH IN US STOCK MARKET" — dramatic phrase with no number. The $360 billion figure is buried in line two. That number should be line one.
✓ number is the hook
"Oil crashed 15.27% in May" — exact number, exact timeframe, historical comparison — all in one line. Reader gets the full story from the hook alone.

Image: flag as second image vs person as second image

✗ South Korea flag adds nothing
Flag tells the reader nothing they don't already know from the text. Wastes the second image slot.
✓ CEO photo adds human context
Jensen Huang's face connects to the NVIDIA story. The person is part of the narrative. That earns the second image slot.

BTC post: two problems in one tweet

✗ cropped chart + wrong number format
Chart top is cut off — highest price levels not visible. Text reads "-4000$" — minus sign and dollar sign both wrong.
✓ fully visible chart + correct format
Full chart visible from bottom to top. All price levels and labels readable. Number format is clean: "15.27%" — no prefix errors.

BREAKING usage: correct vs incorrect

✗ BREAKING on a milestone recap
Taiwan hitting $5 trillion for the first time is significant — but this is a milestone post written after the fact, not live news.
✓ BREAKING on actual live news
NVIDIA crossing a historic market cap threshold in real time is genuine breaking news. BREAKING is earned here.

Pre-Post Checklist

Run through every item before posting. Click to check off. Reset for each new post.
15-point quality check
First line is the single most important or shocking fact — a specific number, not a label
Hook works as a standalone line — someone who reads only this understands the main event
BREAKING is only used if this is live news happening right now
No emoji before BREAKING — the word comes first
All numbers are exact — no "roughly," "around," or approximations
Dollar amounts written correctly — "$4,000" not "-4000$"
Hook uses USD — foreign currency with USD equivalent stays in the body, not the hook
No dramatic closing line added for rhythm or effect
No bullet points in the body — flowing prose only
Checked the chart on a phone screen — fully visible, all labels readable, nothing cropped
If using a second image — it shows a person, product, or relevant graphic (not a flag or generic logo)
Story is on-brand — markets, macro, crypto, AI, or geopolitics
Unverified claims use "reportedly" or "allegedly"
No editorializing for or against any government or company
If late to the story — the post adds unique data, context, or a better visual not covered elsewhere
0 / 15 complete

Build the narrative organically. Introduce the product naturally — never front-loaded. Reader understands the macro context before seeing the product mention.
